31	
32	# DAG Walker
33	
34	You take a **seed declaration** and walk UP its dependency cone in the leandag graph, making the cone **complete**: every declaration the seed transitively depends on must have (1) a real statement block, (2) an informal proof so its effort is finite (not ∞), and (3) a `\uses{}` set that matches the dependencies its mathematics actually uses. You turn ∞-effort cones into finite ones and you make the frontier trustworthy — a node is only honestly "ready to prove" when its `\uses{}` is complete and every dependency is done or itself on the way.
35	
36	You are the dependency-completeness agent. Unlike a blueprint-writer (one chapter, precise directive), you follow the graph wherever it goes — across chapters — filling whatever is missing beneath the seed.
37	
38	## Directive Format
39	
40	```markdown
41	# DAG Walker Directive
42	
43	## Slug
44	<slug>
45	
46	## Seed
47	<the blueprint label to walk up from, e.g. thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus.
48	 May be an ∞-source, the project goal, or a frontier node to make honest.>
49	
50	## Strategy context
51	<the slice of STRATEGY.md the dispatcher extracted — what this cone supports in
52	 the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
53	
54	## Depth / scope
55	<how far up to walk: "the whole cone to the axioms" (default), or "one level —
56	 the seed's direct \uses only", or "stop at chapter X". Plus any out-of-scope
57	 declarations.>
58	
59	## References
60	- <path/to/reference.md or pointer>: <which results live here>
61	```
62	
63	## How you walk
64	
65	Use **`archon dag-query`** (read-only) to navigate — do not eyeball the graph or trust memory:
66	
67	```
68	archon dag-query node      --node <seed> --json      # inspect the seed
69	archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json      # the full dependency closure (everything it transitively uses)
70	archon dag-query gaps --json                         # all ∞ holes, project-wide
71	```
72	
73	`archon` is on PATH. JSON goes to stdout (the banner to stderr), so `--json` is parseable. For each node in the cone, check the **three completeness conditions** below, fix what's broken, and **recurse**: a block you add has its own dependencies — add those too, walking up until you bottom out at a done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) or an axiom (a node with no further dependencies).
74	
75	## The three completeness conditions (per node in the cone)
76	
77	1. **Exists with a statement.** Every `\uses{label}` in the cone must resolve to a real blueprint block. A `\uses{}` pointing at a label that exists in no chapter is a **broken edge** (`archon dag-query` / `leandag build --json` reports these). Fix it: correct the label, or — if the dependency is real but unblueprinted — **add the missing `\begin{lemma/definition}` block** (statement + `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses`) in the appropriate chapter.
78	
79	2. **Has an informal proof — finite effort, not ∞.** A node whose `effort_local` is ∞ is a statement with **no `\begin{proof}`** (and no sorry-free Lean): a roadmap hole. Formalizing it would be blind progress. Give it a proof sketch:
80	   - If the result is genuinely the project's to prove, **write the informal proof** (mathematical prose, project notation, `\uses{}` for each step's dependencies), grounded in a reference per citation discipline below.
81	   - If **Mathlib already provides it**, make it a **Mathlib dependency anchor** (`\mathlibok`, see below) instead of proving it.
82	   - If it is genuinely hard / not-yet-in-the-literature / needs novel mathematics, **do not fabricate a proof** — leave it ∞, record it under "Could not complete", and surface any strategy implication.
83	
84	3. **`\uses{}` is complete — the trust anchor.** Read the node's statement and informal proof, and verify that **every mathematical fact it actually relies on is declared in `\uses{}`**. If the proof of `T` invokes lemma `L` but `T` does not `\uses{lem:L}`, add it — and if `lem:L` has no blueprint block, create it (condition 1) and walk up into it. This is the single most important check: an under-declared `\uses{}` makes a node *look* ready (its missing dependency is invisible to the frontier) when its real foundation is not done. Do not over-declare either: a `\uses{}` to something the proof does not use is noise — remove it only when you are sure it is spurious.
85	
86	   **When the node has Lean code, the Lean side is the ground truth.** Read the matched `.lean` declaration (you read Lean; you never edit it): the facts its proof *actually* invokes — project lemmas it calls, instances it needs — are what `\uses{}` must transcribe, not just what the informal sketch happens to mention. And the project keeps a **1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence**: if the cone's Lean files contain helper declarations with no blueprint entry (`archon dag-query unmatched` lists them), give each one an entry — trivial statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}`, accurate `\uses{}`, one-line proof — and wire its consumers to `\uses{}` it. A helper without tex is an invisible dependency.
87	
88	A node is **complete** when it is `\leanok` / `\mathlibok`, or it has a real statement + a finite-effort proof + a complete `\uses{}` whose targets are themselves complete or on the way. Stop walking up through `\mathlibok` anchors — they are the leaves of the proof-obligation tree.
89	
90	## Markers (same rules as every blueprint agent)
91	
92	- **Never add `\leanok`.** It is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. Not yours.
93	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor.** When the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib already provides, write the statement, point `\lean{}` at the **real Mathlib declaration**, mark `\mathlibok`, and add a `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}` line — no `\begin{proof}` needed. This gives leandag a done node so the `\uses{}` resolves and the effort is finite. **Anti-hallucination rule:** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote. A wrong `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop skips proving a real gap. If unsure it is in Mathlib, leave it ∞ and report it.
94	
95	## Citation discipline (the hard rule)
96	
97	Every block you write that derives from external reference material needs: a `% SOURCE:` comment (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with the **verbatim** original-language statement copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof environment when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` first line. **Never write any of these from training memory.** If you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait for it, open the new `references/<slug>.md`, then write the citation block. If retrieval fails, leave the block flagged and report it INCOMPLETE — never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
98	
99	## What you must / must not do
100	
101	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). The CLI rejects writes outside it. You do NOT edit `.lean` files, `content.tex`, `macros/`, or any state file. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
102	- **Respect protected material** (the "Protected by the mathematician" section of your invocation prompt): never edit a protected chapter or an `all`-protected label's block; a `statement`-protected block may only gain a `proof` environment. Wiring INTO protected blocks is fine — other declarations may `\uses{}` their labels — but their own `\uses{}` lists are the mathematician's; report missing edges there in "Notes for dispatcher" instead of editing.
103	- **Keep every chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
104	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no typeclass notes, no project-history narrative — the blueprint reads as a standalone document.
105	- **Re-query after editing.** Run `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed>` again at the end: the cone should now have zero broken `\uses{}` and (except for declared "Could not complete" nodes) zero ∞ effort. Fix and re-check until it converges or only genuine-gap nodes remain.
106	
107	## Workflow
108	
109	1. Read the directive. Inspect the seed: `archon dag-query node --node <seed> --json`.
110	2. Pull the cone: `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json`. Note which nodes are ∞ (need a proof), which `\uses{}` are broken, and which blocks may be missing.
111	3. Read the relevant chapters on disk and `references/summary.md`; read every reference your directive names.
112	4. Walk the cone bottom-up (axioms/Mathlib first), applying the three checks. Add missing blocks, add missing `\uses{}` edges, write missing proofs or Mathlib anchors. Recurse into anything new you introduce.
113	5. For any block needing a source you don't have locally, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
114	6. Re-query the cone; fix residual broken edges / ∞ holes until only genuine gaps remain.
115	7. Write your report.
116	
117	## Logging
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/dag-walker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).
120	
121	```markdown
122	# DAG Walker Report
123	
124	## Slug
125	<slug>
126	
127	## Seed
128	<label>
129	
130	## Status
131	<COMPLETE — cone fully grounded | PARTIAL — genuine gaps remain (listed below)>
132	
133	## Cone before → after
134	- ∞ holes: <N before> → <M after>
135	- broken \uses: <N before> → <M after>
136	- blocks added: <count>;  \uses edges added: <count>
137	
138	## Blocks added / proofs written
139	- `\label{...}` in `chapters/<slug>.tex` — <one line: statement / why it was a missing dependency>
140	- Proof sketch for `<label>` — <brief shape; ∞ → finite>
141	- Mathlib anchor `<label>` → `\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}` `\mathlibok`
142	
143	## \uses edges added/fixed (the completeness fixes)
144	- `<node>` now `\uses{<dep>}` — its proof invokes <dep> but didn't declare it (made the frontier honest).
145	- Fixed broken `\uses{<bad>}` in `<node>` → `<good>` / created `<dep>`.
146	
147	## Could not complete (genuine gaps — strategy items)
148	- `<label>` — no informal proof exists (hard / novel / not in literature). Left ∞. <strategy implication, if any>
149	
150	## References consulted
151	- `references/<file>.md` — <what verbatim content you took>
152	
153	## Notes for dispatcher
154	- <cross-chapter concerns, needed macros, oversized chapters, etc.>
155	```
156	
157	## Return Value
158	
159	Your final assistant message: one line — `<slug>: COMPLETE | PARTIAL — <one-sentence outcome>` — plus the path to your full report. Keep it short; the dispatcher reads the report.

85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (\cref{def:depth} and \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (\cref{def:projective_dimension}),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (\cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (\cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (\cref{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  \uses{def:depth, lem:ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator}
110	
111	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
112	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
113	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
114	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
115	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
116	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
117	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
119	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
120	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
121	  \[
122	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
123	  \]
124	\end{lemma}
125	
126	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
127	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
128	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
129	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
130	%  By Lemma REF we have
131	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
132	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
133	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
134	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
135	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
136	%  Consider the long exact sequence
137	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
138	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
139	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
140	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
141	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
142	%  Lemma REF.
143	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
144	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
145	\begin{proof}
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	  
150	  
151	\leanok
152	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
153	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
154	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
155	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
156	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
157	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
158	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
159	  -)\). Multiplication by \(x\) annihilates each \(\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) (because
160	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
161	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
162	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
163	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
164	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
165	\end{proof}

167	\section{Projective dimension}
168	\label{sec:ab_pd}
169	
170	\begin{definition}
171	
172	\leanok
173	  [Projective dimension]
174	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
175	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
176	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
177	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
178	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
179	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
180	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
181	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
182	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
183	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
184	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
185	  \[
186	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
187	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
188	  \]
189	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
190	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
191	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
192	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
193	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} concerns only nonzero \(M\).
194	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
195	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
196	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
200	\begin{itemize}
201	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
202	    iff \(M\) is free);
203	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
204	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
205	\end{itemize}
206	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
207	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
208	
209	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
210	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
216	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
217	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
218	  \uses{lem:natCast_add_one_le_of_le_sub_one}
219	
220	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
221	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
223	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
224	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
225	  %  \begin{enumerate}
226	  %  \item
227	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
228	  %  \item
229	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
230	  %  \item
231	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
232	  %  \end{enumerate}"
233	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
234	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
235	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
236	  \(R\)-modules. Then
237	  \begin{enumerate}
238	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
239	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
240	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
241	  \end{enumerate}
242	\end{lemma}
243	
244	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
245	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
246	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
247	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
248	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
249	%  from Lemma REF."
250	\begin{proof}
251	  
252	  
253	  
254	  
255	  
256	\leanok
257	  \uses{lem:depth_via_ext, lem:ext_vanish_of_natCast_lt_depth, lem:natCast_add_one_le_of_le_sub_one}
258	  By \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
259	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
260	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
261	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
262	  \[
263	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
264	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
265	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
266	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
267	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
268	  \]
269	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
270	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
271	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
272	  the proof of \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}.
273	\end{proof}
274	
275	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
276	\label{sec:ab_main}
277	
278	\begin{theorem}
279	
280	\leanok
281	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
282	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
283	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
284	  
285	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
286	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
287	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
288	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
289	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
290	  %  \(\)

365	  \uses{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence, lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd,
366	    lem:depth_eq_of_linearEquiv, lem:depth_pi_const_eq_depth_of_nonempty}
367	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
368	
369	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
370	  finite free resolution
371	  \[
372	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
373	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
374	  \]
375	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
376	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
377	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
378	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
379	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
380	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
381	  sequences
382	  \[
383	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
384	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
385	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
386	  \]
387	  and applying \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} iteratively,
388	  yields the chain of inequalities
389	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
390	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
391	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
392	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
393	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
394	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
395	
396	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
397	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
398	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
399	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
400	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
401	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
402	  \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor \(x \in
403	  \mathfrak{m}\) exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \(M\) as above; the snake
404	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
405	  \[
406	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
407	  \]
408	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
409	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
410	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
411	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
412	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
413	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
414	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
415	  \[
416	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
417	  \]
418	  Substituting yields
419	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
420	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
421	\end{proof}

645	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
646	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}) reads: a complex
647	\(\dots \to R^{r_2} \xrightarrow{\varphi_2} R^{r_1}
648	 \xrightarrow{\varphi_1} R^{r_0}\) of finite free modules over a
649	Noetherian local ring \(R\) is exact at \(R^{r_i}\) (for \(i \geq 1\))
650	if and only if \(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi_{i+1} + \texttt{rank}\,\varphi_i
651	= r_i\) and \(\texttt{depth}\,I(\varphi_i) \geq i\), where
652	\(I(\varphi)\) denotes the ideal of \(r\)-minors of \(\varphi\) for
653	\(r = \texttt{rank}\,\varphi\). The recipe for an iter-201+ prover:

655	\(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi\)-minors of \(\varphi\) (Mathlib supports
656	this via \(\texttt{Matrix.minors}\) and \(\texttt{Ideal.span}\) over the
657	finite index set of \(\binom{n}{r}\) tuples); (b) prove the depth
658	lower bound via induction on \(i\) using a Koszul-complex argument

660	\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow \texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)
661	as a corollary by taking the start \(\dots \to R^{r_1} \to R^{r_0} \to
662	M\) of a minimal resolution and applying the criterion at \(i = 1\).
663	Estimated \(\sim 150-200\) LOC project-side; Mathlib upstream PR
664	candidate.

711	\subsection{Gap (1) first-step substrate (iter-199): per-syzygy
712	  minimal surjection from a finite free module}
713	\label{subsec:ab_gap1_first_step}
714	
715	\begin{lemma}
716	
717	\leanok
718	  [Per-syzygy minimal surjection from \(R^n\) for finite modules
719	    over a local ring]
720	  \label{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
721	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
722	  \uses{def:depth}
723	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
724	  % (lemma-add-trivial-complex; minimal-resolution carving substrate)
725	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L24590-L24623).
726	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
727	  % "Suppose $R$ is a ring. Let
728	  %     \ldots
729	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 1}}
730	  %     R^{n_i}
731	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_i}
732	  %     R^{n_{i-1}}
733	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i-1}}
734	  %     \ldots
735	  %  be a complex of finite free $R$-modules. Suppose that for some $i$
736	  %  some matrix coefficient of the map $\varphi_i$ is invertible.
737	  %  Then the displayed complex is isomorphic to the direct sum of a
738	  %  complex
739	  %     \ldots \to
740	  %     R^{n_{i + 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 2}}
741	  %     R^{n_{i + 1}} \to
742	  %     R^{n_i - 1} \to
743	  %     R^{n_{i - 1} - 1} \to
744	  %     R^{n_{i - 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i - 2}}
745	  %     R^{n_{i - 3}} \to
746	  %     \ldots
747	  %  and the complex $\ldots \to 0 \to R \to R \to 0 \to \ldots$ where
748	  %  the map $R \to R$ is the identity map."
749	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
750	    (\texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}).}
751	  Let \(R\) be a local ring (Noetherian-ness NOT required at this
752	  step), \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) the maximal ideal,
753	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\) the residue field, and \(M\) a finite
754	  \(R\)-module. Then there exist \(n \in \mathbb N\) and an
755	  \(R\)-linear surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\) such that:
756	  \begin{enumerate}
757	    \item \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\) (the minimal
758	      generator count of \(M\), via Nakayama);
759	    \item \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) (the
760	      ``minimality'' condition: every entry of every relation lives
761	      in the maximal ideal, so the chosen lift has no trivial
762	      summands).
763	  \end{enumerate}
764	\end{lemma}
765	\begin{proof}
766	
767	\leanok
768	  Pick a \(\kappa\)-basis
769	  \(b : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \kappa \otimes_R M\) via
770	  \texttt{Module.finBasis} with
771	  \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\). Surjectivity of the
772	  canonical map \(M \twoheadrightarrow \kappa \otimes_R M\)
773	  (composition of \texttt{TensorProduct.mk\_surjective} and
774	  \texttt{Ideal.Quotient.mk\_surjective}) gives lifts
775	  \(m_i \in M\) with \(1 \otimes m_i = b_i\). Define
776	  \(f : R^n \to M\) by \(f x := \sum_i x_i \cdot m_i\) via
777	  \texttt{(Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m}.
778	
779	  Surjectivity: \(\mathrm{range}\, f = \mathrm{span}_R(m_i)\) by
780	  \texttt{Module.Basis.constr\_range}, and
781	  \(\mathrm{span}_R(m_i) = M\) by Nakayama via
782	  \texttt{IsLocalRing.span\_eq\_top\_of\_tmul\_eq\_basis}.
783	
784	  Kernel containment: for \(x \in \ker f\) the relation
785	  \(\sum_i x_i m_i = 0\) tensors to
786	  \(\sum_i (1 \otimes x_i \cdot m_i) = 0\); using
787	  \texttt{TensorProduct.tmul\_smul} and the linear independence of
788	  \(b\) (\texttt{Module.Basis.linearIndependent}) we obtain
789	  \(\mathrm{residue}_R(x_i) = 0\) for every \(i\), i.e.\
790	  \(x_i \in \mathfrak m\). Decomposing
791	  \(x = \sum_i x_i \cdot \mathrm{Pi.single}\,i\,1\) then yields
792	  \(x \in \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) by
793	  \texttt{Submodule.smul\_mem\_smul} and
794	  \texttt{Submodule.sum\_mem}.
795	\end{proof}

799	over a local ring \(R\), it produces a minimal surjection
